sections.title: Predicative Adjectives — No Ending
sections.explanation: Adjectives can be used in two ways in German. A predicative adjective follows a linking verb (sein, werden, bleiben) and describes the subject. Predicative adjectives have NO endings — they stay in their base (dictionary) form. Structure: Subject + linking verb + adjective. Examples: 'Das Haus ist groß.' (The house is big.) 'Die Suppe ist heiß.' (The soup is hot.) 'Der Mann ist müde.' (The man is tired.) 'Das Kind bleibt ruhig.' (The child remains calm.) Notice that 'groß', 'heiß', 'müde', and 'ruhig' all stay unchanged regardless of the noun's gender.
sections.tip: Predicative (after ist/sind/bleibt/wird): NO ending. Das Haus ist groß. Die Frau ist groß. Der Mann ist groß. All just 'groß'!

examples.en: The house is big. (neuter — no ending)
The soup is hot. (feminine — no ending)
The man is tired. (masculine — no ending)
The children are loud. (plural — no ending)

exercises.prompt: What ending does a predicative adjective take in German?
exercises.explanation: Predicative adjectives (after sein, werden, bleiben) take NO ending. 'Das Haus ist groß' — 'groß' has no added ending.

exercises.prompt: Which sentence uses a predicative adjective correctly?
exercises.explanation: 'Die Katze ist klein.' — 'klein' follows 'ist' and has no ending. This is a predicative adjective.

exercises.prompt: Der Kaffee ist ___. (The coffee is hot.) — use 'heiß', no ending
exercises.explanation: 'Der Kaffee ist heiß.' After 'ist', the adjective stays in its base form: heiß (not 'heißer' or 'heißes').

exercises.prompt: How do you say 'The children are tired' (predicative)?
exercises.explanation: Predicative adjective after 'sind': no ending. 'Die Kinder sind müde.' (not 'müden' or 'müdes').

exercises.prompt: All of these nouns have different genders. Which adjective form is correct after 'ist'?
options: Same form for all — no ending
exercises.explanation: Predicative adjectives are invariable: 'Der Mann ist groß. Die Frau ist groß. Das Kind ist groß.' All use 'groß' with no ending.

sections.title: Attributive Adjectives with Definite Articles
sections.explanation: When an adjective comes BEFORE a noun (attributive), it must take an ending that matches the noun's gender, number, and case. With a definite article (der/die/das), the endings are called 'weak endings' because the article already shows most of the gender information. Weak endings: -e or -en. Nominative: der alte Mann (-e), die alte Frau (-e), das alte Kind (-e), die alten Kinder (-en). Accusative: den alten Mann (-en), die alte Frau (-e), das alte Kind (-e). A simple pattern: Nominative singular = -e everywhere. Accusative singular = -en for masculine, -e for feminine and neuter. All plurals = -en.
sections.tip: With der/die/das: singular nominative and acc. fem./neut. = -e; everything else = -en. The -en ending is the 'safe' default.

examples.en: I see the old man. (masc. acc. → -en)

exercises.prompt: What ending does 'groß' take in 'der große Mann' (nom.)?
exercises.explanation: With definite article, masculine nominative: adjective takes -e. 'der große Mann' (the big man).
With definite article, feminine nominative: adjective takes -e. 'die alte Frau' (the old woman).

exercises.prompt: die alt___ Kinder (the old children, plural)
exercises.hint: Plural adjectives with definite article always take -en
exercises.explanation: Plural adjectives with definite article always take -en: 'die alten Kinder'.

exercises.prompt: What is the accusative of 'der alte Mann'?
exercises.explanation: Masculine accusative with definite article: adjective takes -en. 'Ich sehe den alten Mann.' (I see the old man.)

exercises.prompt: Match each noun phrase to the correct adjective ending (with definite article, nominative):
exercises.explanation: With definite article, nominative singular: always -e. Plural: always -en.

exercises.prompt: Complete: 'Ich lese das ___ Buch.' (I read the interesting book.) — neut. acc.
exercises.explanation: Neuter accusative with definite article: -e. 'Ich lese das interessante Buch.' (Neuter acc. with 'das' → -e.)

exercises.prompt: Er kauft den neu___ Wagen. (He buys the new car.) — masc. acc.
exercises.explanation: Masculine accusative with definite article (den): adjective takes -en. 'den neuen Wagen'.

sections.title: Attributive Adjectives with Indefinite Articles
sections.explanation: When the noun phrase has an indefinite article (ein/eine/kein/keine), the adjective must carry more gender information because 'ein' alone doesn't distinguish masculine from neuter (both use 'ein'). These are called 'mixed endings'. The key endings to know: Masculine nominative: -er (ein alter Mann — the -er shows masculine). Feminine nominative: -e (eine alte Frau). Neuter nominative: -es (ein altes Haus — the -es shows neuter). Accusative: masculine gets -en (einen alten Mann), feminine stays -e (eine alte Frau), neuter stays -es (ein altes Haus). Plural with kein: -en (keine alten Häuser).
sections.tip: With ein/eine: the adjective must show gender! Masc. nom.= -er, neut. nom.= -es, fem. nom. = -e. Masc. acc. = -en.

examples.en: I see an old man. (masc. acc. → -en)

exercises.prompt: What ending does the adjective take in 'ein ___ Mann' (old man, nom.)?
exercises.explanation: With 'ein', the adjective must show masculine by taking -er: 'ein alter Mann'. (der alte Mann with def. article uses -e).

exercises.prompt: What is the correct phrase for 'a beautiful house' (neuter, nom.)?
exercises.explanation: Neuter nominative with 'ein': adjective takes -es (to show neuter gender): 'ein schönes Haus'.

exercises.prompt: eine jung___ Frau (a young woman, fem. nom.)
exercises.hint: Feminine with indefinite article — same as with definite article
exercises.explanation: Feminine with 'eine': adjective takes -e: 'eine junge Frau'. (Feminine nom. is -e with both definite and indefinite articles.)

exercises.prompt: Complete: 'Ich kaufe einen ___ Wagen.' (I buy a new car — masc. acc.)
exercises.explanation: Masculine accusative with indefinite article: adjective takes -en: 'einen neuen Wagen'.

exercises.prompt: Match each noun phrase to the correct adjective ending (with ein/eine, nominative):
exercises.explanation: With ein/eine: masc. nom. -er, fem. nom. -e, neut. nom. -es, masc. acc. -en.

exercises.prompt: Which sentence is correct? 'She has a nice cat.' (Katze = fem.)
exercises.explanation: 'Katze' is feminine. Accusative feminine with 'eine': adjective takes -e. 'eine nette Katze' (not -es or -en).

exercises.prompt: Das ist ein gut___ Idee. — Idee is feminine
exercises.explanation: 'Idee' is feminine (die Idee). With 'eine', feminine nominative adjective takes -e: 'eine gute Idee'. (Das ist eine gute Idee.)
